topic: LSP / Code-Intelligence Tool Design Across AI Agent Harnesses and Libraries
purpose: Design-space deep dive for building an LSP primitive for the @agent-sh/harness-* TypeScript library, targeting autonomous agents. Cross-harness comparison across 12 dimensions; adapter-interface recommendation; per-dimension trade-off analysis.

sources.title: Claude Code Tools Reference — LSP tool behavior
keyInsights: The `LSP` tool is first-class in Claude Code with no explicit operation parameter in the schema; operations are inferred from the full-docs description (jump to def, find refs, hover type, list symbols, find implementations, trace call hierarchies).
Automatic diagnostics after every file edit is the load-bearing behavior — not an operation the model calls.
The tool is 'inactive until you install a code intelligence plugin for your language' — plugin-delivered server configuration.
Permission: `No` — the LSP tool doesn't require explicit allow/ask/deny, unlike Bash/Edit/Write/WebFetch.

sources.title: Claude Code Plugins Reference — LSP servers section
keyInsights: The `.lsp.json` manifest schema: required fields `command` + `extensionToLanguage`; optional fields `args`, `transport`, `env`, `initializationOptions`, `settings`, `workspaceFolder`, `startupTimeout`, `shutdownTimeout`, `restartOnCrash`, `maxRestarts`.
Transport defaults to `stdio`; alternative is `socket`.
Available plugins (2026): `pyright-lsp`, `typescript-lsp`, `rust-lsp` — others in the official marketplace via the plugin discovery page.
Users must install the language-server binary separately; manifest only configures the connection.
This schema is a reusable spec — adopt wholesale for library-level plugin compatibility.

sources.title: Claude Code Discover and Install Plugins — Code intelligence section
keyInsights: 11 LSP plugins in the official Anthropic marketplace: clangd-lsp (C/C++), csharp-lsp, gopls-lsp, jdtls-lsp (Java), kotlin-lsp, lua-lsp, php-lsp (intelephense), pyright-lsp (Python), rust-analyzer-lsp, swift-lsp (sourcekit-lsp), typescript-lsp.
Each plugin bundles the manifest but requires the binary on PATH; `Executable not found in $PATH` is the standard failure mode.
Two capabilities Claude gains: (1) automatic diagnostics after edits; (2) code navigation (definitions, references, hover, symbols, implementations, call hierarchies).
Known trouble: language servers like rust-analyzer and pyright can consume significant memory on large projects — disable the plugin if memory-constrained.
Monorepo workspaces: language servers may report false-positive unresolved imports for internal packages — doesn't affect edit ability.

sources.title: OpenCode `lsp.ts` — tool source (experimental)
keyInsights: Nine operations enumerated: goToDefinition, findReferences, hover, documentSymbol, workspaceSymbol, goToImplementation, prepareCallHierarchy, incomingCalls, outgoingCalls.
Schema uses 1-indexed line and character (`min(1)`) — converts to LSP's 0-indexed internally.
Two-layer guards: `assertExternalDirectoryEffect` (workspace fence) + `ctx.ask({permission: 'lsp'})` (permission subsystem).
Fail-fast `hasClients(file)` check with human-language error: 'No LSP server available for this file type.'
Empty result message: `No results found for ${args.operation}` — not an empty array.
Output: `JSON.stringify(result, null, 2)` — raw LSP JSON, which is a design miss; models struggle with nested Location[] ranges.
Gated by `OPENCODE_EXPERIMENTAL_LSP_TOOL=true`; still experimental as of 2026.

sources.title: OpenCode `lsp/server.ts` — language server registry
keyInsights: 40+ languages via per-language `Info` objects: `{id, extensions, root(fn to find project root), spawn()}`.
TypeScript ecosystem: typescript-language-server (TS/JS), @vue/language-server (Vue), svelte-language-server, @astrojs/language-server, vscode-eslint (auto-downloaded from GitHub).
Python: Pyright with venv detection at `.venv`, `venv`, or `$VIRTUAL_ENV`; Ty (experimental) with interpreter discovery.
Aggressive auto-install: go install, gem install, dotnet tool install, GitHub release download + extract to Global.Path.bin — library should NOT copy this.
Java JDTLS requires Java 21+, downloads Eclipse distribution; Kotlin binaries from JetBrains CDN; Clojure via `clojure-lsp listen`.
Auto-install is convenience for end-users; wrong boundary for a distributable library.

sources.title: OpenCode `lsp/client.ts` — LSP client implementation
keyInsights: Sends: initialize (with rootUri/processId/workspaceFolders/capabilities), initialized, workspace/didChangeConfiguration, workspace/didChangeWatchedFiles, textDocument/didOpen, textDocument/didChange.
Tracks open files in `Map<path, version>`; increments version on each change.
Receives `textDocument/publishDiagnostics`; debounces 150ms to coalesce syntax-pass + semantic-pass diagnostics.
45-second initialization timeout, hard.
`shutdown()` ends connection, disposes resources, kills server process.
Does NOT discuss UTF-16 vs UTF-8 position encoding — implicit assumption.
Full TypeScript LSP client is ~300 LOC — feasible for a library.

sources.title: OpenCode `lsp/language.ts` — extension-to-language map
keyInsights: 60+ extensions mapped to language identifiers, dot-prefixed (e.g., `.py → python`).
Compound extensions supported (`.html.erb`, `.js.erb`).
One exception without dot prefix: `makefile → makefile`.
Detection is pure file-extension-based; no shebang, no content sniffing.

sources.title: Language Server Protocol 3.17 specification
keyInsights: Position is {line, character} — both 0-based; character offset encoding is UTF-16 by default (spec-mandated), negotiable via `general.positionEncodings` and `capabilities.positionEncoding` to UTF-8 or UTF-32.
Initialization sequence: client → initialize → server → InitializeResult → client → initialized notification. Requests before init are rejected with `-32002`.
Hover returns `Hover | null` where contents is `MarkupContent | string` (v3.17 narrowed from legacy MarkedString[] union).
Definition returns `Location | Location[] | LocationLink[] | null` — three legal shapes.
References takes `context: {includeDeclaration: boolean}` — model may want/not-want the definition itself.
Rename returns `WorkspaceEdit` which can touch arbitrary files and include CreateFile/RenameFile/DeleteFile ops.
Diagnostics: `textDocument/publishDiagnostics` is server-initiated notification, NOT a request; v3.17 adds optional pull model via `textDocument/pullDiagnostics`.

sources.title: Serena — MCP semantic-symbol toolkit on LSP
keyInsights: Tool surface is semantic, not positional: find_symbol (by name), symbol_overview, find_referencing_symbols, rename, replace_symbol_body, insert_after_symbol/before_symbol, safe_delete.
Dual backend: default LSP-based (40+ languages); alternative JetBrains IDE bridge (all JetBrains-supported languages except Rider/CLion).

Argument: 'Symbolic editing tools are less error-prone and much more token-efficient than typical alternatives. A single rename-symbol call replaces what would otherwise demand 8-12 careful, error-prone steps.'
Server lifecycle: managed launch or HTTP/MCP mode.
Multi-layered config: global defaults → MCP launch params → per-project overrides → context-specific → dynamic mode fragments.
Memory system for long-lived workflow persistence.
Symbol-first design hides position-encoding entirely from the model.

sources.title: mcp-language-server — minimalist MCP wrapper for LSPs
keyInsights: Six tools: definition (returns full source, not just location), references, diagnostics, hover, rename_symbol, edit_file (multi-edit by line numbers).
User-wired binary: `--workspace [path] --lsp [server-executable] -- [lsp args]`. POSIX `--` convention for LSP args pass-through.
Transport: stdio only ('The language server must communicate over stdio').
Supported LSPs: gopls, rust-analyzer, pyright, typescript-language-server, clangd; generic compatibility with any stdio LSP.
`definition` inlines source code — trades tokens for round-trips (single call instead of Location + follow-up Read).
Marked as beta; no explicit limits documented.

sources.title: lsp-mcp — dynamic schema-generated MCP tools
keyInsights: Dynamically generates supported MCP tools from LSP JSON Schema capabilities advertised at initialize.
Zod validation; multiple LSPs can run simultaneously via a single config.
Lazy initialization: LSP servers start on-demand per query, not at MCP server startup.
Node.js implementation; Docker containerization for deployment.
Dynamic tool generation is elegant but wrong for LLMs: models prefer a small, stable, human-curated tool surface over a reflected N-tool surface where schemas drift.

sources.title: Multilspy — the Microsoft Research library for LSP-for-LLMs
keyInsights: Per-operation Python methods (NOT a unified tool): request_definition, request_references, request_completions, request_hover, request_document_symbols.

11 supported languages: Java (Eclipse JDTLS), Python (jedi-language-server), Rust (rust-analyzer), C# (OmniSharp/RazorSharp), TypeScript, JavaScript (typescript-language-server), Go (gopls), Dart, Ruby (Solargraph), Kotlin, PHP (Intelephense).
Auto-downloads platform-specific server binaries; sync + async APIs via context manager.
Powers NeurIPS 2023 Monitor-Guided Decoding paper.
Library shape (per-operation methods) is correct for programmatic callers; wrong shape for a tool surface — convert to discriminated-union at the tool layer.

sources.title: Microsoft monitors4codegen — Monitor-Guided Decoding research
keyInsights: NeurIPS 2023 paper: 'Monitor-Guided Decoding of Code LMs with Static Analysis of Repository Context'.
Key finding: 'MGD can improve the compilation rate of code generated by LMs at all scales (350M-175B) by 19-25%, without any training/fine-tuning required.'
Monitors implemented via LSP: dereference validation, function argument count verification, typestate method sequencing, enum constant generation, class instantiation constraints.
LSP provides ground-truth repository context that constrains decoding — the strongest quantitative argument for LSP-for-LLM.
Limitations: requires running language servers; addresses code-level correctness, not algorithmic correctness.

sources.title: vscode-languageserver-node — Microsoft's LSP TypeScript libs
keyInsights: Four packages: vscode-languageclient (editor-coupled, do NOT use in a generic library), vscode-languageserver (for writing servers), vscode-languageserver-protocol (types, reusable), vscode-jsonrpc (transport, reusable).
For a standalone tool library, use vscode-jsonrpc + vscode-languageserver-protocol directly. Skip vscode-languageclient (it hooks into VSCode's extension APIs).
Lifecycle pattern: `await client.start(); ... await client.stop();`. All handlers return Disposable for cleanup.

sources.title: Aider repo-map documentation
keyInsights: Aider uses tree-sitter + graph ranking (PageRank-style) instead of LSP. Not a tool — the repo-map is included in LLM context.
`--map-tokens` defaults to 1K tokens; Aider dynamically adjusts size based on chat state.
Graph is file-level: each source file is a node; edges are dependencies. Ranking picks 'most often referenced' files.
Output format: file path + `⋮...` elisions + `│` prefix on kept lines, showing class/function signatures only.
No types, no references, no rename — but instant, zero runtime deps beyond grammar bundles.

sources.title: Aider blog — 'Improving GPT-4's codebase understanding with ctags'
keyInsights: 17+ languages via `py-tree-sitter-languages` Python module — pip-installable binary wheels.
Evolution: moved from ctags to tree-sitter for richer AST-based extraction and automatic language support.
Sample output: `filename:` header + `⋮...` + indented signatures prefixed with `│`.
Tree-sitter advantage over ctags: structured AST, no external tool dependencies, permissive licensing on grammars.
No direct comparison to LSP, but the architectural argument is identical: static parsing is strictly weaker than server-based semantic analysis, but much faster and simpler.

sources.title: Cline tree-sitter service — `list_code_definition_names` implementation
keyInsights: Uses tree-sitter, not LSP. 17 supported extensions: js jsx ts tsx py rs go c h cpp hpp cs rb java php swift kt.
Max 50 files per directory.
Three phases: parse → query (language-specific `tags.scm` captures definitions, filtering by `name.includes('name')`) → format.
Output: `|----` separators + `│` prefix on each kept line, sorted by line position, separators between gaps >1 line.
Captures definition names only, not implementations — the AST tells you about structure, not semantics.
Complement to LSP, not a replacement.

sources.title: Cursor agent tools reference
keyInsights: Cursor exposes NO LSP tool to the agent. The IDE owns LSP internally.
Agent gets Semantic Search ('Finds code by meaning, not just exact matches') over an indexed codebase, plus Search Files/Folders, Read Files, Edit Files.
IDE-embedded harness design: the model sees distilled results, not raw LSP ops. Contrasts with autonomous tools where the agent has to own code nav.
Validates: if harness is IDE-embedded, LSP-as-tool is redundant. If harness is autonomous, LSP-as-tool is load-bearing.

sources.title: OpenAI Agents SDK — Tools documentation
keyInsights: Five tool categories: hosted OpenAI (WebSearchTool, FileSearchTool, CodeInterpreterTool, HostedMCPTool, ImageGenerationTool, ToolSearchTool), local runtime (ComputerTool, ShellTool, LocalShellTool, ApplyPatchTool), function, agents-as-tools, experimental Codex tool.
No dedicated LSP or code-intelligence tool ships.
FileSearchTool is retrieval-based over OpenAI Vector Stores — semantic document lookup, NOT code analysis.
HostedMCPTool is the escape hatch: 'exposes a remote MCP server's tools to the model' — users plug in Serena or mcp-language-server externally.
Validates: the ecosystem distributes LSP-for-LLM via MCP, not as first-class framework tools.

sources.title: MCP reference servers catalog
keyInsights: Seven official reference servers: Everything, Fetch, Filesystem, Git, Memory, Sequential Thinking, Time.
None of them is LSP or code-intelligence related — a clear ecosystem gap.
Users compose their own: community-authored mcp-language-server, lsp-mcp, Serena are all third-party.
Distribution opportunity: ship `@agent-sh/harness-lsp` as both a native TS library and an MCP server for wider reach.

sources.title: OpenCode tools documentation
keyInsights: `lsp` tool is experimental; gated by `OPENCODE_EXPERIMENTAL_LSP_TOOL=true`.
Full tool list (non-experimental): bash, read, write, edit, grep, glob, apply_patch, skill, todowrite, webfetch, websearch, question.

Operations published match the nine enumerated in the source: goToDefinition, findReferences, hover, documentSymbol, workspaceSymbol, goToImplementation, prepareCallHierarchy, incomingCalls, outgoingCalls.
Configuration of LSP servers is documented on a separate `/docs/lsp` page; per-project scope.

sources.title: Armin Ronacher — 'Agentic Coding' blog post
keyInsights: Tools must be 'fast', 'user friendly', and 'protected against an LLM chaos monkey using them completely wrong' — applies doubly to LSP where position off-by-one and cold-start are real attack surfaces.
'Anything can be a tool. A shell script can be a tool, an MCP server can be a tool, a log file can be a tool.' — supports the MCP-as-distribution argument.
Go advantage: 'tests run straightforwardly and incrementally, significantly enhancing the agentic workflow' — relates to verification-tool argument.

Does not discuss LSP specifically, but the practitioner stance on 'give the model verification tools it can run' (tests, linters, LSP diagnostics) is the foundation for the diagnostics-via-hook pattern.
